option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is appropriate for focal epilepsy and monotherapy reduces polytherapy risks; suitable if seizure control is stable and GTCS are no longer dominant.
option_3_rationale: Valproate covers generalized seizures and potential mixed seizures; monotherapy reduces drug burden while maintaining broad coverage.

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure free on valproate monotherapy, which is appropriate and effective for generalized epilepsy in this age group; continuing is optimal.

option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy if valproate contraindicated/not tolerated

option_1_label: Continue valproate and topiramate, add clobazam

option_2_label: Continue valproate and topiramate, add clonazepam

option_3_label: Continue valproate and topiramate, add levetiracetam

option_1_rationale: Valproate is appropriate and currently controlling GTCS; previously tried and stopped ineffective or contraindicated drugs should remain stopped; monotherapy continuation is preferred.

option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is broad-spectrum and less sedating, a reasonable alternative if valproate is contraindicated or causes side effects, though more costly and not first-line in this setting.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred first-line ASM for generalized tonic-clonic seizures and offers broad-spectrum control; switching reduces risk related to suboptimal carbamazepine use in generalized epilepsy.

option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a broad-spectrum ASM suitable for generalized seizures and may be considered if valproate is contraindicated or not tolerated, offering a second-generation alternative with fewer metabolic side effects.

option_3_rationale: If valproate is not tolerated or unavailable, phenobarbital monotherapy remains a viable, accessible option in Uganda for focal/multifocal seizures, despite sedation risks.

option_1_rationale: CBZ is first-line for focal seizures; optimizing dose before changing or adding medications is critical given recent breakthrough seizures and prolonged seizure without dose history suggesting need for titration.

option_3_rationale: If CBZ is not tolerated or ineffective, valproate offers broad-spectrum efficacy covering focal and generalized seizures but is second-line here given female child and focal seizure focus.

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure-free on valproate monotherapy, which is first-line for generalized tonic convulsive seizures in young children; best to continue and optimize this effective monotherapy.
option_2_label: Continue valproate, add clobazam if breakthrough seizures occur

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure free on this effective and appropriate regimen for generalized tonic-clonic and atonic seizures with developmental delay; maintaining current therapy is optimal.

option_3_label: Continue valproate, consider phenobarbital addition if seizures recur
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital as a fallback adjunct is often accessible; not first choice due to sedation but can be reconsidered if breakthrough seizures develop.

option_2_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy, stop carbamazepine

option_3_label: Stop carbamazepine and consider lamotrigine monotherapy
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is an alternative if ethosuximide and valproate are contraindicated or not tolerated; requires slow titration but acceptable in absence seizures.

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure-free for 2 years on valproate; monotherapy is optimized and effective despite focal seizure reclassification.
option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is first-line for focal seizures and may better target the current seizure type; appropriate to switch if desire seizure-type specificity.
option_3_label: Continue valproate, add clobazam if breakthrough occurs
option_3_rationale: Retain effective valproate monotherapy but keep clobazam available as affordable adjunct for possible breakthrough seizures without changing baseline therapy.

option_1_rationale: Given mixed or evolving seizure types and partial response, continuing the current polytherapy regimen is reasonable to maintain broad coverage while monitoring efficacy and side effects.
option_2_label: Continue valproate, stop carbamazepine, start lamotrigine

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure-free on valproate, which is appropriate for generalized seizures including status epilepticus history; continue effective monotherapy and stop previously tried levetiracetam.

option_3_rationale: Maintain current regimen while monitoring for cognitive and speech development impacts, as valproate is currently well controlling seizures; reassessment before considering adjunct or switch.

option_1_label: Continue valproate and carbamazepine, add clobazam

option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure free on valproate monotherapy, the first-line broad-spectrum drug for generalized tonic-clonic seizures; continuation is appropriate and preferred.
